topic.what: Systematic comparison of individual-level allocation (ILA) vs unit-level allocation (ULA) strategies under differential privacy constraints for welfare-maximizing resource allocation
topic.how: Develop private variants of ILA and ULA using Joint zCDP, derive regret bounds across three settings: observable welfare, costly sampling, and predictive modeling with auxiliary features
topic.why_matters: Provides first general framework for navigating tradeoffs between privacy, efficiency, and targeting precision in fair allocation systems; reveals ULA dominates ILA when budgets are small, data is expensive, inequality is high, or welfare is hard to predict

characteristics.what_it_measures: Regret of private algorithms relative to non-private baselines as problem size grows
characteristics.context: Price of privacy negligible when budget Theta(population)
Depends on budget fraction, Gini coefficient, mean welfare, and prediction error

characteristics.what_itasures: Relative cost of measuring welfare vs allocating aid

characteristics.what_it_measures: Whether prediction-based approach outperforms pure sampling
characteristics.value: Modeling stronger when lambda large; conditions depend on M, E*, lambda
characteristics.context: ULA has less to gain from modeling than ILA because unit-level targeting already leverages information

characteristics.what_it_measures: Sensitivity to irreducible Bayes risk sigma^2
characteristics.value: ULA ignores sigma^2 in expectation; ILA accumulates it linearly
characteristics.context: ULA averages predictions across units, washing out irreducible uncertainty

good_for: scenarios where individual welfare is hard to predict from features
not_for: low-inequality populations where individual targeting is efficient
settings with cheap data collection where sampling-based ILA is viable
domains requiring Nash or egalitarian welfare ordering
situations where treatment effects depend non-linearly on unobserved factors
requires: differential privacy infrastructure for mechanism design
population can be partitioned into meaningful units
administrator can observe or sample welfare scores at cost
welfare scores bounded in [0,1] with homogeneous treatment effects

novelty: First systematic framework for private allocation with clean interpretable bounds
Joint zCDP algorithms for both ILA and ULA with budget satisfaction guarantees
Characterization of three asymptotic regimes based on inequality, sampling cost, and budget
Analysis of modeling vs pure sampling tradeoffs in private allocation
Extension of Shirali et al. framework to privacy-constrained settings
